ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for yam cake; mapped to steamed cake food product as closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Yam cake (steamed, rice flour and taro-based savoury cake)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for yam cake; A0CKP (cereal-based savoury snack) used as closest defensible anchor. Documented as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for yam cake or taro cake as of 2026.

allergens: gluten (rice flour may be cross-contaminated; dried prawns are crustacean shellfish)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of yam cake is one piece weighing approximately 120 g, commonly served as a breakfast item or snack at hawker centres in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Cake — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yam Cake (steamed), CR ID F11090067. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Taro / Yam-based Steamed Cake — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (taro, cooked) and peer-reviewed literature on rice-flour-based steamed cakes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Singaporean yam cake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from: (1) cooked taro GI ~55–64 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); (2) rice-flour-based steamed savoury cakes GI ~65–72 (peer-reviewed literature); weighted composite estimate of 67 (medium) is defensible given the primary carbohydrate sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 34.1) / 100 ≈ 22.8 for a 120 g serving (carbohydrate = 28.4 × 1.2 = 34.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 436 mg per 100 g with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Yam cake contains 436 mg sodium per 100 g — approaching the upper limit of a single snack's sodium budget for hypertensive individuals (target ≤600 mg/meal); patients should limit portion to half a piece and request reduced soy sauce and salt during preparation, as the pan-fried variant adds further sodium from garnish sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 28.4 g/100 g with medium GI 67 and low sugar 0.2 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yam cake has a medium GI of 67 and moderate carbohydrate content of 28.4 g per 100 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22.8 per standard 120 g serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to one small piece and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium 436 mg/100 g and moderate fat 2.4 g/100 g from dried prawns and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (436 mg/100 g) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; total fat is low at 2.4 g/100 g and saturated fat is estimated at 0.6 g/100 g, which is acceptable, but the sodium burden means cardiovascular patients should treat yam cake as an occasional snack and avoid the pan-fried version which significantly increases fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 436 mg/100 g exceeds 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sodium at 436 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for rheumatoid arthritis management, potentially worsening tissue swelling; the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or significant antioxidants means yam cake provides no anti-inflammatory benefit and should be consumed sparingly by RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam cake (芋头糕, Yu Tou Gao) is a traditional Singaporean savoury steamed cake made from grated or diced yam (taro), rice flour, dried prawns (hae bi), garlic, shallots, and seasoning including soy sauce and five-spice powder. The mixture is steamed until firm, then sliced into rectangular pieces and typically served at room temperature or lightly pan-fried to create a golden crust. The texture is dense and slightly sticky with a mild earthy yam flavour and a savoury umami depth from the dried prawns. It is commonly garnished with fried shallots, spring onions, chilli sauce, and sweet sauce. Yam cake is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, popular as a breakfast item or afternoon snack. Nutritionally, it provides 145 kcal per 100 g with moderate carbohydrate (28.4 g), low protein (2.5 g), and low fat (2.4 g). Its primary clinical concern is high sodium at 436 mg per 100 g, making it unsuitable for hypertensive and rheumatoid arthritis patients without portion restriction. The medium glycemic index of approximately 67 means individuals with type 2 diabetes should practise portion control. Calcium (13 mg) and iron (0.3 mg) are present in small amounts. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to dried prawns, and is not certified halal due to potential cross-contamination in traditional preparations.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11090067), which is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, documented transparently. Multilingual names verified against standard Singaporean Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi culinary references.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean yam cake preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11090067) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent product as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g based on the fat profile of comparable rice-flour steamed savoury cakes in USDA SR Legacy (closest approved analogue). GI of 67 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for Singaporean yam cake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimate derived from cooked taro GI range (55–64) and rice-flour steamed cake literature (65–72). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (67 × 34.1) / 100 ≈ 22.8. Halal status is unconfirmed for hawker-prepared versions; dried prawns are crustacean shellfish (allergen). FoodOn URI mapped to steamed cake food product as closest ancestor; FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as approximate anchor — no dedicated code exists for yam cake in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
